data.title: Dycom Industries (And Me) To The AI/Data-Centers Bubble Bears: Just Saying 'No!'
data.description:
The 1980s and ‘90s called—They want their wires, coaxial cables, and fibre optics back.

Z-z-z-z.

That’s how I reacted when I first looked at a business description for Dycom Industries, Inc. (DY).

But upon further review, I see good things.

The stock rates quite well in Seeking Alpha. Its Quant Rating is Strong Buy. I like that. And as we’ll see below, this rating is very well deserved. The Wall Street rating is also a Strong Buy. The SA Analysts Rating is Buy.

Beyond that, I like shares of companies with boring businesses.

So, too, does legendary former Fidelity Magellan Fund (FMAGX) manager Peter Lynch. Chapter 8 of his classic One Up on Wall Street was titled “The Perfect Stock, What a Deal.”

He discussed 13 perfect stock traits.

Number one consists of companies having dull or ridiculous names. DY doesn’t count here. But it does fit the runner-up trait, “It Does Something Dull”

The idea is to invest at prices that haven’t been elevated by favorable publicity or sentiment. We can wait till later on, when prices are boosted by earnings.

My appreciation for boring businesses led me to a bunch of stocks in the Engineering and Construction Industry.

So much the better if boring businesses are also very profitable and growing briskly. That works for Engineering and Construction…

It’s easy to see our infrastructure is aging. So, there’s more and more business coming to companies working to upgrade and/or maintain it.

But I hadn’t thought of DY’s bailiwick, “the planning and design of aerial, underground, and buried fiber optic, copper, and coaxial cable systems,” etc. (quote from the Seeking Alpha Company Profile) as a standout within the industry.

Such activities proceeded briskly in the latter part of the 20th century. Companies worked hard to roll out cable TV, broadband internet, and mobile communications. They spoke of building an “information superhighway.”

Congratulations to the industry!

It succeeded. The superhighway exists. We routinely burn rubber today as we speed along this highway.

So why get excited now, past the quarter mark of the 21st century? It was a great story. But is it still fresh current?

There are two affirmative answers. Both contribute to bullish investment cases for DY:

  1. The traditional wiring business still has far more life than many realize, and
  2. A recent acquisition makes DV a serious AI-Data-center play.

So let’s call DY boring-and-then some.

Wiring in 2026 and Beyond

At a 12/9/25 conference, CEO Daniel Peyovich introduced the company in a way that matched my basic assumption.

We're across all 50 states. We do both wireline and wireless infrastructure for the major telecommunications and cable customers. We're across all 50 states. We do both wireline and wireless infrastructure for the major telecommunications and cable customers…

Yes indeed, an obvious 20th-century business.

Or not…

Peyovich may have been anticipating my sensibilities when he continued thusly:

I think that we often hear that people believe because it's been going on for some time now that it's already reached some kind of pinnacle or it's already starting to decelerate when, in fact, the opposite is true. Most of the customers that we have today are continuing to ramp their programs. There's obviously a lot of bullishness around getting fiber deployed.

The number, if you take the passings collectively across our customers, if you look at the total homes in America, it gets to about 80% roughly or about 120 million, 125 million passings. So there's still a ton of work to do, and that's going to continue to ramp in the coming years….

[T]he passings, the number of passings per year are going to continue to grow over the next several years. You certainly have -AT&T has been very vocal about their continued growth. You have many other customers that fall into that as well.

But DY charges by the foot, not by the home. And the spaces with which it deals are getting more expansive.

Lower-hanging fruit (e.g., 40-foot lot lines) was built out long ago. Today, DY is dealing with more expansive lot lines, especially as it wires more rural areas. And the company today is dealing with more (expensive) underground installations.

Underscoring the importance of getting everybody everywhere wired (thinking of broadband as an essential utility), we have a U.S. program known as BEAD (Broadband Equity and Access Deployment). It’s administered by the NTIA (National Telecommunications and Information Administration).

This could eventually bring $26 billion in new revenue to DY for fiber or HFC (hybrid fiber coaxial) cable. None of this has yet hit DY’s revenues or backlog. But these multiyear outlays are likely to start kicking in during the fiscal year starting 2/1/26. DY has already received a $500 million verbal award.

And by the way, it’s not only about new installations. We need a lot of work on the existing infrastructure. As Peyovich said at the 12/9/25 conference:

You have infrastructure that's been there for 25, 30 years. The capacity is even close to what they're looking for today. And very importantly, the latency is not even in the ballpark. And they also need redundancy. So if something does get hit in the field, you don't want to take down their entire cloud, their entire network. So they are looking for redundancy as well.

It sounds here like Peyovich is talking about bigger things than home installations. That’s so. He’s referring to…

connecting ultimately data center to data center across the country, you're talking about tens of thousands of miles.

So, I was wrong. The wiring business is much bigger—and more contemporary and stretching at least into the 2030s—than I realized.

All in on Data-centers

Did you notice, in the last quote, the reference to connecting data centers? It’s been top of mind for DY.

Connecting data centers is all well and good. We know how important these are.

But there’s more to be done than just connecting them. How about getting inside the fence—moving from the public right of way to inside the actual data center campus.

That’s exactly what DY did when, on 12/23/25, it completed its acquisition of Power Solutions.

The latter is an electrical contractor that focuses on doing work in data centers. And within this sphere, Power does a lot.

This, according to DY, amounts to a compelling adjacency.

Take a look at the revenue numbers at the bottom of the above image. Financially speaking, Power Solutions is transformative for DY.

The purchase is expensive:

The purchase price on a cash-free, debt-free basis was comprised of approximately $1.63 billion of cash, including an estimated working capital adjustment, and approximately 1.0 million shares of Dycom common stock.

But DY expects the acquisition to immediately be accretive to earnings.

More importantly, it makes DY a major player in data-center construction.

Schematically and superficially, though, one has cause to wonder..

DY has a nationwide presence. Power Solutions, on the other hand, does most of its work in a single region.

Geographically, Power Solutions looks measly, almost like a pimple on an elephant.

But if Power Solutions is to be mainly in one region, oh what region that is!

It’s known as DMV (D.C, Maryland, Virginia). Some refer to part of the territory as “data center alley.” That’s because it accounts for more than 35% of overall U.S. datacenter capacity.

Juxtaposing the single Power Solutions region with the nationwide DY presence suggests a big opportunity for DY to cross-sell data-center electrical contracting.

That’s exactly how DY sees it. Note the title of the above slide. Note too the cross-selling aspirations expressed below.

This appears to be a great move for DY!

But it does force us to squarely confront a serious and controversial issue…

Talk of an AI/Data Center Bubble

It’s no secret that a lot of folks harken back to the turn-of-the-century telecom bubble and predict a repeat. Given the way DY just went very much all in on data center construction, anybody who is bullish on this stock has to definitively reject the bubble scenario.

I am among those who see bubble talk as misguided.

It’s different this time—Yes, I said it. Many love to attack those they catch saying things like this.

History means something, the bears insist. Maybe it repeats. Maybe it rhymes. Either way, ignore it at your peril.

I agree!

History, or as I prefer to say, precedent, is important. But that’s so only if, if, if you analyze it correctly.

What exactly is it we should expect to repeat or rhyme?

Bears point to the turn-of-the-century grotesque telecom overbuilding and the horrifying correction that ensued and destroyed much shareholder wealth, including, famously, the bad things that happened to those who owned shares of big suppliers like Cisco (CSCO).

But that is the wrong precedent (history) to consider.

Lawyers, who intensely study and live under stare decisis, the doctrine of precedent, will tell you precedent is meaningful if, and only if, the key facts are similar.

That is not even remotely so in the case of CSCO and circa-2000 telecom.

I, who was already a veteran analyst back then and had a front-row seat to all of it, discussed this in considerable detail in my 1/24/26 report on Sterling Infrastructure (STRL).

It would be a lot to repeat here in its entirety. So I strongly, very strongly, urge you to read that write-up if you have any interest in DY.

Briefly, very briefly, the telecom companies that failed back then were, pardon me for speaking bluntly, commercially useless. They had to fail. Their business models were dumb and resulted entirely from political activism. There was no consumer or enterprise demand.

Nobody today who has used AI in a grown-up way sees it along those lines. Real AI users laugh at the garbage (my opinion) coming out of Magnificent 7 conference rooms and know that stuff doesn’t deserve to ever make money.

So we couldn’t care less about how observers today complain nobody is profiting from AI. (Actually, though, Palantir (PLTR) is profiting handsomely.)

Legit AI users understand what really works and are already seeing serious benefits emerging out of the grassroots, from pioneers and little companies you haven’t yet heard of, just like at one time, nobody ever heard of Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), or Alphabet’s (GOOGL) Google.

The real (repeatable, relevant) precedent is the way tech as a whole (computers, mobile, Internet, etc.) has evolved with new previously-unknown winners and far, far more value creation than even the early bulls dared imagine.

DY’s Numbers

These are going to look pretty good. (Remember, the Seeking Alpha Quant Rating is Strong Buy.)

Bear in mind, though, that most of these numbers do not reflect Power Solutions. The deal closed after the latest period included in the historic numbers.

But given the likely impact of Power Solutions, consider many of these numbers as bases upon which DY is likely to build.

Let’s start with quarterly revenue and EPS trends.

There’s no cause for complaint here!

Let's now move on to the historic and forward growth rates.

(I compare DY to medians among companies in the Construction and Engineering industry and in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.)

How about those historical growth rates!

They look about even with the already-strong industry medians. But remember, they don’t include Power Solutions. They reflect only the businesses I erroneously assumed were 1980s–’90s relics.

The core fundamentals also reflect only pre-Power Solutions business.

Here, too, DY gives a good account of itself,

Finally, here are the Financial Statement Highlights.

The table looks fine.

But as we consider the future, we have to be realistic. The Power Solutions purchase will add a lot of new revenue, gross profits, and operating profits. But it will also add a lot of new debt and equity.

The main thing to consider here is whether the company’s history suggests DY appears to have been good enough at financial management to justify an assumption it will be able to handle its large new acquisition. I believe the answer is “yes.”

Risk

Obviously, when a company makes a huge acquisition, we have to be alert to possible integration issues and operating challenges.

Here, at least, DY management seems to be very much wired into (pun completely intended) the need to absorb lots of new people.

Indeed, Peyovich said on the 11/19/25 earnings call:

With the addition of Power Solutions talented team of over 2,800 to our current team of over 16,100, we will have a combined highly skilled workforce of 19,000 people. This is a massive competitive differentiator that enables us to meet the growing needs across our collective customers.

It seems DY went into this as ready as any acquirer has ever been to integrate a large new workforce.

And, of course, there’s the risky elephant in the room — whether or not an AI/data-center bubble is poised to burst.

I’ve explained above and here why I do not believe this to be so. But everyone is entitled to their opinion. If you disagree with mine, DY is not a stock for you.

What to Do About DY Stock

Here are DY’s valuation metrics.

On a PEG basis, DY looks like a bargain compared to the SPY group—higher growth, lower PEG. Of course you have to believe in the growth projection. I do, especially with the addition of Power Solutions.

The price chart is intriguing—and jarring.

Let’s start with the basics. The rising 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) is above the also-rising 50-day EMA. Neither looks like a moonshot. But both are OK. So from a momentum standpoint, DY looks OK, albeit not spectacular.

Now let’s examine Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trade is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Yikes!

Look how deeply negative these indicators have recently been.

Sellers being more motivated than buyers acts as a drag on stock prices. And when we’re seeing so much negativity in CMF, we have to pause and consider why. Marc Chaikin, creator of CMF, describes it as a measure of “smart money” behavior.

It’s not a perfect signal. I’ve seen as much in past backtests I’ve done on it. But it still is an important clue. If we’re to trade against the smart money, it behooves us to at least know why.

A lot, I believe for DY, involves institutional nervousness about the large Power Solutions purchase.

I’ve long observed investor discomfort with big acquisitions. It’s almost as if institutional investor job applicants are asked, on an entrance test, “Do you fear big acquisitions?” If they answer “No,” the application is automatically rejected.

And here, for added urgency, DY’s big acquisition flies directly in the faces of the bubble bears.

So I get it.

I’m usually leery of going against powerful, smart money sentiment. But as noted, they aren’t perfect.

I’ve given this matter careful consideration. And I believe that here, we have an opportunity to buy before the smart money recognizes how well-positioned DY really is for where the world is going. (It’s analogous to the Peter Lynch dull-company investment case—Buy before “they” figure things out and rush in.)

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding, or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew "Strong …" extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think Buy, Hold, or Sell are enough.

I see DY as likely outperforming the broader 3- to 5-year market.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating DY as a Buy.

data.title: Uncertainty May Lead To A Longer Fed Hold
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Federal Reserve Open Market Committee voted to keep the fed funds rate the same at 3-1/2 to 3-3/4 percent. The move snaps a three-consecutive-meeting streak where the committee had lowered interest rates by ¼ percent in each meeting. Two members dissented with the committee, each wanting a ¼ percentage point reduction. I believe that future rate changes by the Fed are not a foregone conclusion, and we may discover later this year that we have reached the neutral interest rate.

What The Market Predicts: Fed Fund Futures

The Fed fund futures market is a market where traders can place their bets as to where the Fed funds rate will be in the future. It helps give investors an idea as to where the market expects the Fed funds rate to be in the future. Since the last meeting in December, the fed fund futures for January barely moved, staying near the current midpoint of 3.63%.

In translation, nobody was surprised to see the Fed resume holding interest rates. Additionally, the dissent of Trump appointees wanting lower rates is no surprise either, as the Administration continues to call for lower interest rates. Looking ahead, the committee forecasted one more rate cut in 2026, with an average consensus interest rate of 3.4%.

A cut by the Federal Reserve would, in theory, lower the Fed funds rate to 3.33%, or between 3-1/4 and 3-1/2 percent. The Fed fund futures market is currently projecting that interest rate cut to occur in September with the fed funds rate sitting at exactly that level. I believe with the inflation rate above 2% and falling (but not too fast) combined with a slow jobs market (but still creating jobs), the Fed is in no hurry to conduct additional rate cuts.

Looking Ahead: The New Chair

It’s obvious at this point that there will be a new Federal Reserve chairman in May, when Jerome Powell’s term expires. This leaves him with two more meetings as chairman before he is replaced. With a majority of the committee being less dovish than the Trump appointees, I would not be surprised if rates are being held to try to offset the potential of multiple rate cuts with a new chairman and/or additional appointees.

I think the idea of rates holding until at least Powell’s exit is a done deal unless job growth goes negative and/or the inflation rate dives and threatens to push towards zero versus the long-term policy rate of 2%. While job creation has been low, the committee noted that “the unemployment rate has shown some signs of stabilization.” The labor market was the singular reason that the committee began cutting interest rates last fall.

Government Shutdown? Here We Go Again

While it was not on the table a week ago, a new government shutdown appears to be inevitable. Senate Democrats have stated they are not interested in supporting a new appropriations bill that funds the government from February through September. The result is likely to be another government shutdown.

While I have not gotten an explicit definition of whether or not data gathering will be affected by the shutdown, I doubt that the BLS or BEA got the same priority of SNAP benefits when a bipartisan deal was reached several weeks ago. The likely shutdown will once again impact economic data and could come as soon as next Friday’s scheduled January jobs report.

Conclusion

If a government shutdown occurs, it could further incentivize a data-dependent Federal Reserve to keep interest rates the same. The March meeting, which is next, will include a series of forecasted updates for where the Fed sees inflation, unemployment, and the Fed funds rate. Investors will need to look for changes, but based on the likelihood of a government shutdown, I believe data delays and confusion lie ahead.

data.title: Sabre: Shares Implode While Some Green Shoots Emerge
data.description:
Shares of Sabre Corporation (SABR) have seen a dreadful performance as shares of the travel technology business have fallen to lows of just $1 and change. This marks a dreadful performance from an IPO price at roughly 10 times that amount, in an offering that took place more than ten years ago.

This situation has been the outcome of a period of underperforming business performance and a toxic combination of too much debt. Improvements in the core business and a substantial divestment and reasonable prices create some air and time, as investors are no believers in a turnaround, even as some minor green shoots are appearing here.

An Issue Of Debt & Stagnation

Sabre Corp provides trust to the travel ecosystem, being the center of an ecosystem that connects travel suppliers, travel sellers, IT solutions, distribution, and travel buyers.

The business generated about $3.0 billion in sales in 2024 from providing such solutions, and while this number was up minimally, adjusted EBITDA margins improved more than 5 points to 17% of sales. Instrumental in this is a $2.7 billion travel solutions business, with the distribution-part of this involving some 363 million bookings, on which average booking fees approached $6 per share, while the IT solutions served nearly 700 million passengers that year.

The second, and much smaller, business was a $327 million hospitality solutions business, barely making up over 10% of sales, with this business, among others, handling 128 million central reservation system transactions.

While such numbers look impressive, the issue is that this was already a $3 billion business a decade ago, with revenues peaking at $4 billion pre-pandemic, as the company never recovered. While nominal sales are similar to a decade ago, the issue is that of real revenue declines, factoring in the impact of inflation. This was seen with GAAP operating profit margins falling from the higher-teens to the lower-teens. Worse, about 40% dilution has been incurred over this period of time.

The real issue, which created a drag on the business, was that of leverage, a substantial amount of this, and expensive interest rates, leaving no realistic earnings. This made shares essentially a melting ice cube as the company never delivered on its promises.

2024 As A Base

In February of last year, Sabre reported a 4% increase in 2024 sales to $3.03 billion, with adjusted EBITDA up 53% to $517 million. That says very little, as the company incurred an interest bill of around half a billion with adjusted losses reported at $72 million and GAAP losses reported at $278 million, dreadful numbers, even as both improved meaningfully.

Some 385 million shares outstanding traded at just $2 per share, for a market value of just about $800 million, minimal in relation to EBITDA. The issue was that of a $4.3 billion net debt load and over $5.0 billion gross debt load, with effective costs of debt being very high, around 10%, or just above that.

The company aimed to address this as it announced a $1.1 billion deal to sell the hospitality business to TPG Capital in April, with net cash proceeds seen at $960 million, sufficient to reduce net debt by about 20%. With revenues reported at $328 million in 2024, segment margins are seen at just over 10% of sales, as multiples fetched look very reasonable at just over 3 times sales. Activities were valued at a high-twenty times segment EBITDA multiple, as the news sent shares up to $2.50 per share.

Hereafter, reality kicked in with first quarter sales down 1%, the company issuing new bonds over the summer at coupons of 11% and change. Second quarter sales fell another 1%, but third quarter revenue growth returned to positive 3%. Through the first nine months of the year, revenues are flat at $2.1 billion, which is for the remaining travel solutions business, with EBITDA margins for the period up another 2%. Adjusted net losses for the period narrowed from $46 million to $10 million. While this looks encouraging, it, among others, excludes a $35 million stock-based compensation expense.

Year to date, interest expenses of $332 million were basically at par compared to 2024, but this likely will come down with net debt down to $3.7 billion, still highly demanding with adjusted EBITDA seen around $530 million, for a 7 times leverage ratio. This is recognized by the credit marks, with new bonds still carrying 11% and change in terms of the coupons, as issued in November.

What Now?

Trading at just $1 and change, the enterprise valuation of Sabre stands at just over 1 times sales, at about 8 times EBITDA, with no realistic earnings to show for it. Of course, the company did announce (and closed) on a solid sale last year, with the hospitality business sold at around 3 times sales, while its margins were lagging. Continued improvement in the margins and reduced interest expenses make it realistic that losses are rapidly approaching break-even levels and might even turn positive, which is actually encouraging.

This comes as investors are skeptical about this turnaround, and perhaps more skeptical about the competitive positioning of the solutions provided by Sabre. This comes after the business posts flattish sales in a booming air travel market, with AI, and other software developments, of course, hurting prospects for what essentially is a legacy booking/IT/software ecosystem.

The set-up is interesting, with the business performance, notably the margin improvement demonstrated upon, net debt coming down, and expectations only falling. That makes it so that the situation is improving, which is a long way from saying that appeal is seen. For that, the hole for the company to dig out of is huge, as real questions can be asked about the strength of the business model. The model of being a middleman in a world that is getting more transparent is a key risk, alongside other technologies developing, as topline sales growth is lagging the market and peers, as evidence of that.

This makes me very cautious to get involved just yet, but it is the setup for improving bottom-line performance and a lower share price, which makes me inclined to keep an update on the firm into 2026.

data.title: Wall Street And Main Street Are Seeing Two Different Economies
data.description:
Winter storm Fern dumped significant amounts of snow and brought far lower than average temperatures across the country over the weekend. The storm left at least ten dead in New York City and is expected to at least ding first quarter GDP growth.

Another huge weather system is right behind Fern and will affect most of the country this weekend. Miami is expected to see mid-30s as lows on Saturday and Sunday. Here in Delray Beach, one will have to be alert for frozen iguanas falling out of trees (yes, that does happen here).

While most of the nation suffers through a huge economic disruption and freezing temperatures, the S&P 500 hit another all-time high on Tuesday. It occurs to me this divergence is the perfect analogy for two different U.S. economies being experienced right now. The one Wall Street sees, and the one most of Main Street is experiencing.

The S&P 500 has enjoyed three straight years of at least 20% performance. Only the third time in its history, the index has done so. Meanwhile, consumer confidence levels just hit their lowest readings in a dozen years. Lower than even during COVID lockdowns.

The chart above perfectly captures the complete divergence from Wall Street and Main Street. While two thirds of Americans are living "paycheck to paycheck," Wall Street will collect record bonuses from 2025.

Thanks largely to the accelerating globalization following China's entry into the World Trade Organization in 2001 as well as productivity improvements ushered in via the birth of the Internet, corporate margins have expanded significantly in the 21st Century.

Of course, higher corporate profits have largely at the expense of workers over the past 25 years. And this helps explains the increasing political tensions across the U.S., which has helped put a populist in the White House and a socialist in as mayor of New York City. Even the consumer sentiment readings are deeply divergent based on party affiliation, just as they were from 2021-2024 in the opposite direction.

One of the many risks I think investors are overlooking are the rising political dysfunction in the nation. After the coming mid-terms, 2027 could be a year of impeachments and a continual stream of government shutdowns. Not exactly a good omen for the markets or the economy.

The stock of Amazon (AMZN) is trading at record highs. Meanwhile, the company just announced its latest waves of layoffs despite nearly 30% profit gains in FY2025 on sales growth of roughly 12%. The U.S. economy is increasingly one where the rich get richer, a bifurcation also seen in the market with the Magnificent Seven capturing most of the gains across equities since ChatGPT debuted late in 2022.

The top 10% of American households by income now drive roughly half of all consumer spending in the U.S. This is up from the mid-30s in the early 1990s. I think investors need to be aware of this growing bifurcation when making decisions about what goes into their portfolios. It helps explains why Walmart (WMT) and Dollar Tree (DLTR) are prospering as consumers increasingly trade down to make ends meet while most retailers are struggling. As are many restaurants. Corporate bankruptcies jumped 14% in 2025 despite strong GDP headline numbers.

This is why luxury home builders like Toll Brothers (TOL) are still seeing year-over-year revenue gains while mass builders like Lennar (LEN) and housing related concerns like Home Depot (HD) are experiencing sales declines.

So, who is right? Wall Street or Main Street? The consumer is starting to benefit from the lowest gas prices since 2021, and average rents have fallen across the U.S. for five straight months now. Average wage growth has outpaced inflation since 2024 after failing to do so from 2021-2023. A period that saw the CPI top out at 9.1% in June of 2022. Tax refunds should also be up approximately $100 billion from the previous year, roughly $1,000 more per tax filer. This should provide at least a marginal boost to consumer sentiment and spending. Hopefully it also boosts the personal savings rate, which is at historically low levels (above).

On the Wall Street side, equities are trading at extreme valuation levels viewed through numerous traditional valuation metrics like the Shiller PE ratio which is currently north of 40. And even this metric does not capture the impacts that stock buybacks have had on EPS growth. Over 25% of the rise in EPS across the S&P 500 over the past 15 years has come not from organic profit growth, but stock repurchases.

Maybe we should turn to the commodities market to "break the tie" on how the U.S. economy is actually performing. And as you can see below, gold, silver, platinum, and other commodity prices aren't signaling economic stability. In fact, they're showing the largest one-year price increases since 1979-1980, a stagflationary period that was hardly a good time for the U.S. economy or the markets.

data.title: Papa John's International: Trading At 44% Discount To Prior Apollo Bid
data.description:
Introduction

Papa John's International (PZZA) has consistently traded at a discount to peers and the last few years it's found itself struggling to stay competitive. As part of a multi-year turnaround, the company has been challenged by a weak consumer environment in North America but the last few quarters have been showing strength internationally. With lower comps due to a softer demand from lower-income customers, the income statement looks unimpressive compared to last year. That said, with plans to extract savings across the P&L, I think the valuation is going to look quite attractive once it starts seeing savings in G&A reduction and supply chain optimization. With shares trading at a discounted valuation relative to peers and lingering private market interest from prior bids, I believe the current setup appears to offer investors an attractive risk-reward opportunity as the company prioritizes franchise economics, cost discipline, and profitability.

Recent Results

Papa John’s came in with a weak Q3 that highlighted the challenging environment they’re facing with respect to their turnaround. On both revenue and EPS, the company missed by a wide margin. For the top line, sales came in at $508.1 million, missing sellside estimates by $16.6 million and on EPS, the company’s $0.32 missed by 9 cents.

While the turnaround at Papa John’s is still in its early innings, I think investors have punished the stock because of some mixed overall outcomes. On the one hand, Global comparable sales remained flat year-over-year, with North America seeing a 2.7% decline primarily due to reduced orders from lower-income customers and weaker demand for non-pizza items such as sides and desserts. In contrast, international comparable sales surged 7.1%, driven by robust growth in key regions like Europe, the Middle East, and Asia Pacific.

With good performance abroad and weak performance in North America, Global system-wide restaurant sales climbed to $1.21 billion, reflecting a 2% increase on a constant currency basis. While the $508.1 million revenue figure I mentioned earlier was up 0.4% compared to last year, it was nearly entirely driven by International growth (a contribution of an increase of $6 million).

Shifting to margins and profitability, EBITDA edged down slightly by 4.3% to $47.7 million, because of a $4 million increase in additional marketing expense and $2 million in increased compensation. In the company’s domestic-owned restaurant segment, the company-owned restaurant segment's EBITDA margin dipped to 2.4%, a 20bps decline. Gains from higher average tickets were largely negated by lower transaction volumes and ongoing labor inflation.

On a brighter note, the North American commissary segment's adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 7.4%, up 100bps thanks to increased pizza volumes with 3% more pizzas sold compared to the prior year. In my view, these metrics underscore the resilience in core operations despite broader pressures, but it does show that pricing has been coming off a bit compared to last year.

Regarding the balance sheet, Papa John’s financial position is solid with total available liquidity standing at $502 million from cash and credit facilities. Leverage was manageable at 3.4x (far from breaching any covenants). With leverage at 3.4x supported by a business generating ~$200 million of EBITDA, the company shouldn’t have any balance sheet issues. As illustrated below, most of the debt is in the form of senior loans and notes that have an average coupon of 4%, indicating that the company carries a low cost of capital. The 3.875% Senior Unsecured Notes due in 2029 trade for 96 cents on the dollar for a 5.125% yield, suggesting that refinancing at low rates should be attainable.

In terms of some of the key highlights in the quarter, it shouldn’t be understated how well the international business is doing. This was the segment’s fourth consecutive quarter of positive comps with sequential gains and was amplified by new innovations like the Croissant Pizza expansion to three new markets, which sparked media attention and sales lifts. In North America, core pizza sales were held flat despite a 3% uptick in pizzas sold and 4% more per order, as consumers favored medium pizzas with fewer toppings amid budget constraints. Loyalty membership also added nearly one million members to bring the total to 40 million with higher redemptions and frequency.

Outlook

Papa John's updated 2025 guidance strikes me as deliberately conservative, and for good reason. The company is clearly signaling that North American consumer softness (especially among lower-income and small-ticket customers) won’t vanish quickly, so they’ve baked in a continued 2-2.5% comp decline in that market while only modestly lifting international expectations to 5-6%. The EBITDA range of $190-$200 million feels appropriately cautious given the need to lean harder into promotions and incremental marketing to defend share in a hyper-competitive QSR space.

To me, this isn’t pessimism so much as realism, but it’s really about turning this business around for the right reason and prioritizing franchise economics over chasing aggressive near-term targets. That’s especially the right call when we all know that the economic backdrop is uncertain.

Even so, I see more upside potential than the guidance implies, particularly as we move into 2026 and beyond. While North America will likely face a few more bumpy quarters with accelerated store closings trimming the weaker tail of the system, I think the real story is the growing list of self-help levers the company is pulling. The $50 million in supply-chain savings targeted by 2028 (with $20 million landing next year) and the at least $25 million in non-marketing G&A reductions planned for 2026-2027 are meaningful. I think that’s particularly true in a business where every basis point of four-wall improvement flows through nicely. Rather than posting sky-high numbers, I think the conservative guide embeds in some procurement wins, freight optimization, and a leaner corporate structure which should all be attainable given that these are by and large within the company’s control.

I think the outlook gets more interesting when we look longer term. As cost discipline takes hold across the P&L (supply chain, G&A, and eventually better mix from reimagined sides and new platforms) the company should start generating more durable earnings expansion in 2027 and beyond. International momentum in priority markets continues to build, and the accelerated refranchising program will leave Papa John’s with a leaner, more franchise-heavy footprint that’s better positioned to scale efficiently. As well, the balance sheet gives them plenty of room to invest without overextending, so the path to higher margins and better returns is realistic.

Risks

The top risks to the Papa John's story boil down to three key areas: fierce competition in a promotional QSR landscape, the potential for international momentum to slow, and execution slip-ups on the multi-year transformation plan.

Competition remains intense, with larger players and value-focused chains aggressively discounting to grab share. Any escalation could force Papa John's into deeper promotions that squeeze four-wall margins or undermine the premium positioning they've worked hard to rebuild. On the international side, the current growth has been impressive but if macro pressures hit emerging regions harder, that tailwind could fade faster than expected and leave the business more exposed to North American weakness. The company as a whole has been lucky with International softening the declines at home. Finally, the transformation itself on G&A cuts and supply chain has risks since delays in hitting those $50 million and $25 million targets could slow margin expansion and delay the earnings inflection investors are counting on.

Valuation

Even with the risks, I think you’re getting the right price to take on Papa John’s through its turnaround. Most of the sellside is bullish on the stock with seven ‘buy’ ratings, 11 ‘hold’ ratings, and no sell ratings (source: Bloomberg). With an average price target of $46.73, the implied upside of 27.5% from today’s levels plus a 5.1% indicates strong return potential.

Speaking of the dividend, Papa Johns’ has a great track record of more than a decade of growing its dividend and at $0.46 per quarter, the payout ratio seems high on TTM numbers ($1.84 of dividends per year against $1.43 of EPS generated in 2025), but given my view that EPS probably hits over $2 per share in 2027, it’s likely this dividend is safe.

When it comes to the valuation, Papa John’s currently trades for 9.1x EV/EBITDA and 19.5x P/E. Compared to peers at 11.1x EV/EBITDA and 17.5x P/E, I consider the valuation to be quite attractive (source: Bloomberg).

It’s also worth pointing out that the private market value of this business is worth a lot more than the current share price. In June, Apollo Global along with Qatari-backed Irth Capital Management submitted a $2 billion bid (at $64 a share) to take the company private. While Apollo backed out in November which sent shares lower, today’s share price of $36 represents a 44% discount to the offer in June and I do think it’s possible an interested party could come along in the near future. Regardless of whether that happens or not, I think even small steps through the P&L of turning the business around by taking out some costs will set it up well. At a depressed valuation, shares of Papa John’s look cheap.

data.title: AIS: Capex Trends Support A 'Buy'
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

Everyone knows the cycle of artificial intelligence that the world has lived (and lives) in the last few years. That has caused rivers of ink to run by analysts and experts of all kinds, trying to anticipate, scrutinize, or undervalue this technology and its more than potential impact, both in our lives and in the companies that develop and implement it. One way to invest in this technology is to do it in an indexed way, through the VistaShares Artificial Intelligence Supercycle ETF (AIS).

The main objective of this ETF is to assess the capital of its investors in the long term, managing and investing it actively in a portfolio designed by the management team. As they mention in their ETF brochure, “The portfolio is made up of global AI companies, who derive their revenues from the production of high-performance semiconductors, from the creation and management of AI-based data center applications.”

As the reader will see, it is a thematic ETF, with a clear bias towards AI and with a great sensitivity to the CapEx cycle, as we will see later. The investment universe is fairly straightforward to understand, as it can be exposed to the semiconductor industry or companies building some kind of AI hardware. Companies involved in the construction of data center infrastructure or software and applied AI companies are also investable.

Regarding the investment thesis, I recommend buying the vehicle today for the following reasons and making the relevant clarifications. As we will see later, the consensus of analysts regarding investment in capex (hyperscalers) in 2026 is that the leaders of the technology sector will spend more money this year than was thought only a few months ago. This implies an increase in the order lines of the main manufacturers and, consequently, an increase in the EPS forecast. As the ETF is fully exposed to such companies, this will be a differentiating factor in their profitability.

Add to that the market continues to reward the hardware and infrastructure industry (SOX/SPY ratio), and you can be closer to saying that the AI rally is more likely to continue on an upward profit revision than on a simple multiple expansion.

ETF Overview

Analyzing the most operational part of the ETF, we can see that it has an expense ratio of 0.75%, a somewhat high figure, although consistent to pay for the active management performed by the vehicle. The bid/ask spread is 0.43%, a figure also high, which directly harms the investor with a short-term investment horizon. The origin of this may be in the ETF's small volume, because it is quite recent (12/03/2024).

If we look at the second table, the reader may see too many low ratings (D). They are not, as standard deviation and annualized volatility are justified by the category in which the fund itself falls (semiconductors). In the Seeking Alpha image, the comparison is made against the entire universe of ETFs analyzed. The one-year tracking error is a rather pointless piece of data in this case, since, as we mentioned earlier, the fund is actively managed and does not try to replicate the behavior of any market index.

In the table above we have both the performance of the NAV and the market price of the ETF analyzed. We can clearly observe how in the short term (1M) the market price has yielded slightly less than the ETF NAV (2.44% vs 2.55%), while in a longer period (1Yr) it is the NAV that yields less than the market price (58.35% vs 58.20%). With this data in hand, we can see the inefficiencies generated by the high bid/ask spread (0.43%) that we discussed earlier.

The Portfolio

When we looked at the ETF portfolio, we came up with some very interesting information to understand its dynamics and behavior. In the image above, in the sectoral exposure, the bias to the technology sector is shown above left, with a current exposure of 86.34% versus 13.66% in the industrial sector. If we look at the exposure by country, we find that it has a distinctly American bias, allocating 58.91% of the total ETF equity to that country. The United States is followed by Taiwan and China with weights of 14.94% and 6.49%, respectively.

Both the market cap exhibition and the industry exhibition reveal the rest of the details we need to know. ETF has a bias to large caps (55.94%) and another bias to small caps (24.42% vs. 19.64% mid-caps). As I mentioned in the first section, they dominate the semiconductor industry with a weight of 35%, followed by the hardware and storage industry with 20% and communication equipment (13.75%). With this in hand, we can say that, in effect, we are dealing with a book-themed ETF.

In the top 10 assets owned by the fund, we have shares like SK Hynix (Korea) with a weight of 7.41%, which has yielded approximately 300% in the last year. It is followed by Micron Technology Inc. (5.38%) or Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (4.69%).

The CapEx Cycle

The graph above shows the evolution of the market consensus that measures CapEx estimates for 2026 by the major hyperscalers. As can be seen from the graph, the red line (2026) shows a gradual increase throughout the year 2025. This tells me that the market has been changing its thinking about the AI investment cycle from a transient cycle thinking to a structural market cycle thinking. For the ETF that we analyze today, this is a very important variable, since this increase in investment expectations in CapEx benefits the companies that have the ETF in their portfolio, enlarging their order lists, increasing their margins and impacting their earnings (EPS). This is the first argument why I propose to buy the ETF analyzed.

Macro Drivers

Analyzing the main macroeconomic drivers, we see in the first image, how the z-score of the 10-year real interest rate is at a high level, compared to its historical average. This does not favor the ETF at the macro level, as it hinders the expansion of multiples, especially in growth assets, since a higher real rate increases the discount rate that is used to calculate the valuations of these companies. But it is not all negative, because because it hinders multiples expansion, it makes the ETF more dependent on real growth at the level of profits of the very companies to which it is exposed. If they grow, the CapEx cycle discussed above will benefit the fund.

Finally, we observe that the z-score of the ratio (SOXX/SPY) is currently marking a price of two standard deviations, higher than the S&P500 index. This means that the market leadership remains in the hands of these technology companies and that he continues to reward them against other equity sectors, making real the narrative of the AI investment cycle. Looking ahead and with the expectations discussed above, I believe that this difference in the behavior of both indices will be maintained or widened, benefiting AIS. "Buy" 5%-10% of your allocation and don't fall behind. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Amphenol's Q4 Plunge Creates A Reasonable Entry Point, But I Have Long-Term Concerns
data.description:
Sell the good news? Amphenol Corporation (APH) has plunged by double digits after a strong quarterly report and optimistic guidance. It appears some investors had priced in even more optimism, and some investors may also be looking to cash in on profits. The steep selloff creates a solid entry point, by my estimates, and while the stock is a bit expensive by some valuation metrics, with the AI data center build-out likely to continue, if not accelerate, I don’t believe the high valuation metrics are unjustified. If APH hasn’t bottomed out already, I believe it will shortly.

Amphenol sells a variety of connectors, cable assemblies, power source components, and other products in high demand amid the race to build out data centers. Right now, the company is enjoying high margins, and while I expect those margins to eventually come under pressure, with the AI race so hot, it should keep them high for now. That said, I do have concerns over how strong APH’s moat is. Components have a tendency to become commoditized and for margins to eventually sink.

APH also has a strong presence in the defense sector and is known for providing durable solutions in harsh environments. With spending on defense likely to expand not just in the United States but globally, this bodes well for APH. Long track records and a reliable brand can be especially important for defense applications.

APH Quarterly Results and Valuation Considerations

APH’s Q4 Non-GAAP EPS weighed in at $0.97, beating estimates by 3 cents, while revenues of $6.44 billion beat expectations by more than $200 million. If I had to guess why beating estimates led to a sharp sell-off, it’s probably because APH beat estimates by 13 to 15 cents last time around (pictured below). I’d wager that investors priced in a much bigger beat.

We have seen APH rise steadily throughout January (pictured below). Once again, I suspect this price increase was driven by unreasonable expectations for the quarterly results. Now that the recent price drop has eliminated much of the gains over the past few weeks, however, I believe APH represents a much better deal. Short interest also remains low, suggesting that while some may doubt upside, the stock likely isn’t widely viewed as overpriced.

According to the Wall Street Journal, with share prices at $146.19, the P/E ratio comes in at about ~48. This is well below the +55 P/E ratio we were seeing before the recent selloff. While a P/E ratio of 48 isn’t cheap, APH has been enjoying brisk growth and strong margins. The risks of an AI bubble forming and popping can’t be ignored, but for now, I believe the data center build-out will continue (more on that in the next section).

As for the quarterly results, the GAAP Diluted EPS of $0.93 in Q4 is up 58% YOY, while the Adjusted Diluted EPS of $0.97 is up a blistering 76%. The 26.8% GAAP operating margin and 27.5% Adjusted Operating Margin are excellent as well. Sales expanded by nearly 50% in Q4 in U.S. dollars and are up 37% organically from the year prior. With sales totaling $6.4 billion in Q4, this isn’t a small start-up going through a rapid expansionary stage, which helps make the high P/E ratio much more palatable.

The full year numbers look just as good, with the $23.1 billion in sales representing growth of 52% in American dollars and 38% organically from 2024. Operating margins for the full year were a touch lower at 25.4% (GAAP) and 26.2% (adjusted), hinting at rising profitability. The GAAP Diluted EPS of $3.34 marks 74% growth from a year prior, while the adjusted diluted EPS was up 77% to $3.34. Add in the solid dividend payments, with dividends declared per common share totaling 25 cents in Q4, and I believe this is an attractively valued stock.

My fellow Seeking Alpha analysts give APH a Buy rating as well, but the Quant tools give a Hold rating, with the high valuation getting an F score. While I'm a bit more optimistic, the high valuation is a risk investors should be mindful of.

Expect Data Center Build-Out To Continue

APH’s forward guidance is rather bullish, with Q1 2026 revenues expected to total $6.90 billion to $7.00 billion. This marks strong growth, but I believe the bullish outlook is warranted based on both past performance, and the ever-expanding AI data center race. Despite concerns of an AI bubble forming, spending on data centers has only been accelerating in recent months.

Meta Platforms (META) just announced a $6 billion deal with Corning (GLW) to supply a variety of components for data centers over the next several years. Anthropic is raising revenue projections by 20% to $18 billion in 2026, according to reports. Mordor Intelligence recently released a report suggesting that the data center market in Europe will expand by a CAGR of ~26% from 2026 to 2031. This makes sense given the increasing emphasis of reducing reliance on American tech companies. Nvidia reports accelerating AI spending in the financial services industry based on strong early returns.

For now, I expect AI spending to remain strong. A strained energy grid in the United States may encourage diversification, but APH has been generating more than half of its revenues from international markets. As we see below, APH has regularly beaten expectations in recent quarters. During the Q3 earnings report, APH’s guidance suggested that sales for Q4 would come in at $6 billion and $6.1 billion, but sales performed much better at $6.4 billion. As far as I can tell, the management team seems to set moderate expectations, making the positive forward guidance a bit more attractive in my eyes.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Amphenol’s products are more commoditized than say GPUs or AI software. I suspect we’ll see competition heat up rather quickly with connectors and cables, and this will likely occur even before the AI race cools off. If and when the AI data center build-out slows down, margins could come under immense pressure. I do think APH could be among the hardest hit stocks should the AI bubble pop, although defense applications may provide some cushion.

Key Takeaway: Sharp Dip Creates Favorable Entry Point, But Be Mindful Of Long-Term Risks

I believe most of the disappointment from overly exuberant investors should already be priced into APH. Further steep losses would be surprising, and there’s a good chance that there will be a moderate rebound. Data center build-out should remain brisk for now, and APH should continue to lock in strong sales. I do have concerns over margins, especially in the long-run, and believe we could see relatively intense competition in the components space in the not-so-distant future, so this is a stock and industry I recommend investors keep a close eye on.

data.title: Canadian Solar Boasts New AI Monetization Opportunity - Rerating May Be Slow
data.description:
I previously covered Canadian Solar (CSIQ) in October 2025, discussing how the company had benefitted from the lifting market sentiments, thanks to the recovering polysilicon spot prices and the likely higher solar module prices.

Despite that, I had believed that the stock was likely to underperform moving forward, attributed to the structural headwinds observed in the US solar sector, Recurring Energy's inherent lack of profitability, the bloated debt from the growing solar/energy storage assets/elevated U.S.-based capex, and the overbought technical indicators.

Since that last Hold rating, I shall discuss why I am finally re-rating CSIQ as a Buy, thanks to the new data center-related monetization opportunities as they leverage their solar/battery storage capabilities during the ongoing AI boom.

This is aided by the discounted valuations and the management highlighting a new 2026 focus on a healthier balance sheet, with the upward re-rating nearer to its power generation peers likely to trigger rich capital appreciation prospects.

CSIQ May Be an AI Beneficiary Ahead

Since my last Hold rating, the bulls have proven my bearish thesis wrong indeed, with numerous solar-related stocks rallying by the double digits since late October 2025, CSIQ included.

Their tailwinds are multifaceted, in my opinion, attributed to the Chinese government stepping in to "crack down on cut-throat competition and overcapacity in the solar manufacturing sector, as the industry struggles with a prolonged downturn."

The early success has been observed in the notable recovery in the global polysilicon price averages to $7.29 per kg by January 21, 2026, up by +30.8% from the $5.57 per kg observed in the July 2024 bottom - with it effectively marking the end of the solar price war, as similarly highlighted by a key polysilicon producer, Daqo New Energy (DQ), in the recent earnings call.

So with the recovery of market prices across the solar PV value chain in the third quarter of 2025, we believe the industry is gradually recovering from a cyclical downturn. In particular, the polysilicon sector reached an inflection point during the quarter with prices rebounding significantly. (Seeking Alpha)

This is significantly aided by the ongoing AI boom, with the aging utility infrastructure struggling to keep up with the hyperscalers' power-hungry data center build-outs, as numerous "data center developers estimate up to 2 years for grid power access."

Here is where numerous alternative power providers, including solar, nuclear, gas-powered utility plays, and on-site power generators, amongst others, have emerged as near-term beneficiaries, attributed to their supposedly faster deployment timelines and/or modular add-on capabilities and/or lower carbon footprints, as similarly reiterated by CSIQ in the last earnings call:

The rise of AI-driven data center is fueling unprecedented global electricity demand. As I have emphasized in my public speeches over the past 2 years, the most flexible and cost-effective solution for powering data centers is solar-plus-storage. In contrast, traditional energy sources such as natural gas and nuclear power require long construction cycles and have limited scalability. (Seeking Alpha)

Here is where CSIQ's vertically integrated offerings across...

  • CSI Solar, "focused on solar modules and battery energy storage manufacturing and products," and
  • Recurrent Energy, "focused on utility-scale solar power and battery energy storage project development and operation"

... have been able to benefit from the ongoing power "gold rush," as similarly observed in Recurrent Energy's growing total battery energy storage project development pipeline to 80.57 GWh in FQ3'25 (+21.8% YoY).

This is on top of the robust e-STORAGE contracted backlog, including contracted long-term service agreements, at $3.1B (-3.1% YoY), aided by the expanded Manufacturing Capacity Expansion Plans for SolBank Battery Energy Storage Solutions [BESS] to 24 GWh (+60% YoY) and Battery Cells to 9 GWh in 2026 (+200% YoY).

As a result of these promising developments and the excellent growth insights from the higher multi-year backlog, it is unsurprising that CSIQ has already rallied by +239.7% from the 52-week lows or by +426.4% between the 52-week lows/highs.

This is especially since the management has delivered operating discipline through the reduced expenses by -42.6% QoQ/-10.1% YoY in FQ3'25 to record a robust GAAP operating income margin of 2.3% in FQ3'25 (compared to 0.01% in FQ3'24), nearer to the 2.4% observed on a YTD basis (+0.3 points YoY).

Readers must note that CSIQ has also acknowledged its elevated debt leverage, as observed in their strategy in balancing "the growth of our operating portfolio and selective project ownership sales to prudently manage cash flow and debt levels," with 2026 expected to bring forth more "project ownership sales to enhance cash recycling."

This reason is also why readers may expect its total debt, including financing liabilities, to remain stable at or moderate from the $6.4B reported in FQ3'25 (+18.5% YoY), with the potentially reduced net interest expenses from the current levels of $29.34M (+43.5% YoY) likely to improve its bottom lines as well.

On the other hand, readers must note that CSIQ's project ownership sales and deleveraging process is likely to be lumpy and/or prolonged, with the solar company likely to remain unprofitable in the intermediate term, as observed in the adj EPS performance of -$0.58 in FQ3'25 (-87% YoY) and -$2.18 on a YTD basis (-2,080% YoY).

The same has been observed in the consensus forward estimates, with the consensus FY2026 GAAP EPS estimates of $1.00 likely to be negated by the annualized FQ3'25 preferred dividend impact at ~$0.80 per share.

Valuations Still Compelling Despite Recent Rally

For now, despite its lack of near-term profitability prospects, CSIQ remains compellingly valued at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.38x, compared to the sector median of 3.52x, despite the notable upgrade from the 5Y mean of 0.70x and the 10Y mean of 0.69x.

Given that the CSI manufacturing segment still comprises 95% of its overall FQ3'25 revenues and 6.6% of its income from operations (before eliminations), it is unsurprising that the stock remains inherently discounted compared to its Independent Power Producer [IPP] peers with more diversified power generation portfolios across solar, wind, nuclear, natural gas, storage, and others, including:

  • Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. (BEP) at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 12.02x,
  • NextEra Energy, Inc. (NEE) at 9.81x, and
  • Clearway Energy, Inc. (CWEN) at 11.85x,

while being rated closer to its solar panel producer peers, including:

  • Jinko Solar at 0.76x,
  • Longi Green Energy Technology at 1.26x,
  • Tongwei at 1.44x,
  • GCL Technology Holdings (GCPEF) at 3.41x, and
  • First Solar (FSLR) at 4.87x.

This development implies CSIQ's discounted Recurrent Energy prospects - one that is somewhat deserved, since there remains a notable distance (approximately 4 years) until the segment becomes a top-line driver, where "until our IPP business scales further, near-term profitability will continue to depend primarily on global project sales."

As a result, I am of the opinion that the stock is likely to underperform expectations in the intermediate term, especially given baked-in exuberance from the recent rally.

CSIQ is Upgraded to a Cautious Buy

For now, CSIQ has lost much of its recent gains, albeit well defended at the November 2025/January 2026 bottom of $20s, with it implying a new trading floor if the buying momentum continues.

CSIQ Technical Indicators

This conjecture is not overly aggressive as well, based on the reasonable technical indicators across the RSIs and trading volumes, with it implying that CSIQ may chart a decent upside potential from current levels, assuming that the management beats the consensus forward estimates in the upcoming FQ4'25 earnings call sometime in March 2026.

For reference, the current FQ4'25 revenue estimates of $1.37B (-8% QoQ/-9.8% YoY) and adj. EPS estimates of -$0.90 (-55.1% QoQ/+38.77% YoY) imply a sequentially weaker performance, with the low bar implying a potentially outsized rally upon a top- and/or bottom-line beat, similar to the market's reaction post FQ3'25 earnings call.

Combined with the management hinting at their ongoing data center-related engagements across numerous customers in Spain and the US, I am of the opinion that CSIQ may be suitable for those seeking to tap into the multi-year data center/electrification growth trends.

As a result of the improved margin of safety from the growing solar/battery demand despite the changing US regulatory landscape, the correction of -35.9% from recent highs, and the established new trading floor at the $20s, I am cautiously upgrading the CSIQ stock as a Buy.

This is, albeit with the caveat that the stock is only suitable for those with a longer investing trajectory and a higher risk appetite, attributed to the Recurrent Energy segment's prolonged monetization opportunity and the elevated short interest ratio of 15.97% by the time of writing.

data.title: January Fed Meeting: A Steady Start To An Expected Volatile Year
data.description:
U.S. investors have much on their plate to chew on. Quarterly earnings season is in full swing, the government appears to be barreling towards another shutdown, and the fate of tariffs is still in the hands of the Supreme Court. These are all important variables for consideration. But these are backburner items to today’s just-concluded Federal Reserve (“Fed”) January meeting, at least for today.

U.S. equities have largely maintained gains, with the S&P 500 (SPY), Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI), and the Nasdaq Composite (NDX) all continuing to mostly rise on the backs of a strong earnings season. Exceptions include UnitedHealth (UNH), which cratered 20% following the Trump administration’s announcement of underwhelming reimbursement rates for Medicare Advantage payments in 2027.

Today’s Fed meeting, which largely met expectations, will likely keep markets on their current trajectory, for the time being at least. While volatility may arise from the additional catalysts mentioned above, I do believe Fed policy will remain at the forefront of investors minds in the months ahead. Here’s everything you need to know about the January Fed meeting.

How Were Markets Performing Prior To The January FOMC Meeting?

Heading into today’s FOMC meeting, U.S. equities have maintained their gains despite a steady flow of mixed economic data.

U.S. equities have largely shrugged off the mixed economic data, and this has been supported in part by a strong quarterly earnings season. In recent days, for example, major indexes pushed to fresh highs. In my view, this shows that investors believe economic growth, while moderating, is far from stalling out.

Previously, the December CPI release helped some nerves on the inflation front, in my view. December CPI rose 2.7% YOY, unchanged from November and in line with expectations, while core inflation cooled to 2.6%.

For markets, the takeaway was straightforward. Inflation continues to drift lower, and tariff-driven price pressures have been far more muted than feared earlier in 2025. Ahead of today’s decision, that likely gave the Fed some breathing room and reduced the urgency for any shift in policy.

On the flip side, the labor market showed clearer signs of cooling. The December jobs report showed just 50K added, capping the slowest year of job growth since 2003 outside of recessions. While the unemployment rate ticked down to 4.4%, wage growth and hours worked softened. This was supportive of the view that the labor market is in a “low hire, low fire” environment.

Taken together, markets entered the meeting balancing slowing labor momentum against easing inflation.

What Was Expected For The January FOMC Meeting?

Expectations were fairly well set prior to today’s meeting. The Fed was widely expected to pause rate cuts after delivering three consecutive reductions since September.

With the policy rate already lowered into the 3.5%–3.75% range, officials appeared content to step back and let the data do the talking. At today’s meeting, the bigger question wasn’t whether the Fed would hold steady, but what it would take to get them cutting again.

I believe that markets essentially understood the Fed was stuck between two incomplete stories. One was that inflation has indeed cooled meaningfully but still remains stubbornly above the 2% target. At the same time, job growth has slowed sharply, though the unemployment rate has stabilized.

Neither side of the dual mandate had exactly deteriorated or improved enough to initiate a force in the Fed’s hand. As a result, investors expected only modest tweaks to the policy statement, with language that kept the door open to further adjustments later in the year without committing to a timeline.

Aside from the actual rate decision, most were considering the looming Fed chair transition and Powell’s recent flare-up with President Trump. With Powell’s term ending in May, markets have been increasingly focused on who might lead the Fed next. I’ve previously opined to a SA Asks roundtable that Kevin Hassett would be President Trump’s most likely pick. While no announcement was necessarily expected this week, the uncertainty itself added another variable for investors watching the Fed’s next move.

What Changed Following The January FOMC Meeting?

At the surface level, the January policy statement looked quite similar to the December statement. Dig a little deeper, though, and there were a few notable shifts worth paying attention to.

Most immediately, the tone of the policy statement subtly firmed. For example, the committee upgraded its description of economic activity from expanding at a “moderate pace” in December to a “solid pace” in January. It’s a small tweak, but an intentional one, in my view. I believe the change shows that policymakers are signaling a more neutral stance and reinforcing the idea that the economy is no longer behaving like one in need of urgent policy support.

The bigger development came in the form of two dissents. Governors Stephen Miran and Christopher Waller both voted in favor of a quarter-point rate cut, breaking from the broader consensus to hold steady. That split matters. Not only does it highlight ongoing disagreement within the committee, but it also offers a glimpse into how the Fed’s internal dynamics could evolve once Chair Powell’s term ends in May.

Waller’s dissent, in particular, is significant, given his status as one of President Trump’s finalists to lead the Fed. With Trump’s pick still pending, I believe the dissents show a growing divide between policymakers favoring patience and those pushing for a faster pivot toward easing.

Main Takeaways From The January FOMC Meeting

At the end of the day, today’s FOMC meeting delivered a steady hand and very little policy drama. The Fed held rates unchanged, and just as importantly, left its policy language largely intact. There was no rush to declare victory on inflation, no panic over the cooling labor market, and no attempt to pre-commit to the next move. For investors, this consistency is likely much appreciated.

Chair Powell’s post-meeting remarks were notable as well. Against a noisy political landscape and a very public clash with President Trump, Powell returned to his deliberate and measured self. He made it clear that the Fed remains data-dependent, focused squarely on its dual mandate, and insulated from political pressure. In my view, that message was intentional. Powell appears committed to running out the clock on his remaining term without deviating from the framework that guided last year’s rate cuts.

From a market perspective, I believe today’s meeting showed that Fed policy remains the primary macro anchor in what is shaping up to be a volatile year. Earnings, fiscal uncertainty, and tariff risk will all come and go as catalysts, but the trajectory of inflation and the labor market will continue to dictate policy expectations. In my view, it’s apparent the Fed is signaling patience, and unless the data meaningfully shifts, that mindset looks set to remain in place.

data.title: Stifel Financial: Poised For A Growth Inflection
data.description:
Shares of Stifel Financial Corp. (SF) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 9%. The company is benefiting from growth in its wealth management business, and in Q4, institutional activity exceeded expectations, helping to drive an earnings beat. Given the quality of its earnings, I view its valuation as quite attractive at 13x forward earnings and view shares as a “Buy,” especially given its strong capital position.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Stifel Financial earned $2.63 per share, which beat estimates by $0.12 as revenue grew 15% to $1.56 billion, a new record. For the full year, the company earned $6.76 on $5.5 billion of revenue. Pre-tax margins surpassed 22% as we are seeing improving operating leverage. Its wealth management business is benefiting from rising market levels while an improved M&A outlook is lifting investment banking activity, trends that should continue. Plus, Stifel maintains an excellent capital position, facilitating ongoing balance sheet growth.

Markets Lift Wealth Management

Its global wealth management business generated $933 million of revenue, up 8% from last year. Client assets reached $552 billion, up 10% from last year. Of this, fee-based assets reached $224 billion, up an even stronger 16%. This is a key metric, as these assets provide recurring revenue, as a fee of AUM, typically charged quarterly. This revenue is fairly predictable (though it moves a bit with the market), providing the type of steady cash flow that supports dividends and buybacks, thereby leading to a higher multiple on earnings than from non-fee-based sources. Given the strength of the market, we should see a further sequential rise in fee revenue in Q1.

This strength in AUM drove a 12% increase in asset management fees to $456 million. Transactional revenue was up 3% to $207 million. Beyond this, Stifel also earns interest on custodied client cash balances. While Fed rate cuts reduce floating cash yields, the reinvestment of fixed-rate holdings has helped to offset this pressure, and SF’s net interest margin (“NIM”) has been fairly stable over the past year. Cash balances have grown more slowly than assets, but this is to be expected. When the stock market rallies 5% (lifting AUM), cash holdings don’t automatically go up as well; cash stays the same until clients buy or sell. I expect cash revenue to grow more slowly than AUM as a result in 2026.

M&A Surge Should Be Durable

Institutional revenue (where its traditional investment banking activity sits) was $610 million, up a stellar 28% from last year. The company is seeing particular momentum in health care, which has traditionally been a key sector for the firm. In addition, it has a large advisory business focused on small and regional banks. As the regulatory environment has loosened, we have seen a noticeable uptick in deals. Huntington (HBAN), PNC (PNC), and Fifth Third (FITB) have all done deals in recent months, just to name a few. I expect the pace of bank dealmaking to stay elevated in 2026, which should boost SF’s results.

Indeed, in Q4, advisory revenue surged 46% to $277. The high level of advisory activity also creates more capital-raising opportunities, and that revenue jumped 56% to $171 million. These growth rates cannot be sustained, but I expect the level of revenue to remain strong and continue to grow in 2026, given the favorable M&A backdrop. Transactional revenue was the one soft spot, down 10% from last year, due to a difficult comparison given volatility around the Presidential election that created trading opportunities.

Capital Enables Dividends And Growth

Stifel has an excellent balance sheet with an 18.3% tier 1 common equity capital ratio (CET1), reflecting the light capital needs of its wealth management unit. The company has grown its asset base about 3.5% over the past year while actually boosting its capital by 10bps, which speaks to the strong capital generation from its earnings power. Stifel targets a 10% leverage ratio, leaving it with $560 million of excess capital.

This capital position has supported shareholder returns. This week, Stifel raised its dividend by 11% to $0.51, and shares now offer a 1.6% dividend yield. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 1.7% from a year ago. I expect ongoing buybacks, but using capital to grow the business is likely to be a higher priority. Additionally, the company will be doing a 3:2 stock split in February; a split does nothing to alter the fundamental fair value of the company.

Alongside results, Stifel provided initial 2026 guidance, and it expects revenue to rise to $6-$6.35 billion, growing about 12%, even with $100 million of divestitures. Within this, it expects $1.1-$1.2 billion of NII as it grows its balance sheet by $4 billion by year-end, or about $2 billion on average. These guidance ranges are broadly consistent with existing analyst consensus.

This is a substantial amount of balance sheet growth, more than double what it generated last year. Bank loans grew by $1.1 billion over the past year to $22.4 billion, so its lending operation will need to substantially accelerate to hit this target. Now with much more advisory activity and M&A, there should be opportunities for Stifel to use its own balance sheet to help fund deals. Beyond this, like many other wealth managers, it is aggressively trying to grow its securities-based lending practice, which is generally high margin. Given lower borrowing costs, more M&A, and less macro uncertainty, I expect industrywide loan growth to improve substantially in 2026 vs 2025, but this is still an aggressive target.

Now, as noted above, SF has $560 million of excess capital. Assuming a 7,000 S&P 500, 2 Fed rate cuts, ~$1.5 billion of average balance sheet growth, and ongoing strength in bank M&A, I see SF earning $9.50-$9.85 this year. Assuming $400-$500 million of buybacks, SF should generate ~$350 million of capital after buybacks and dividends. Its balance sheet expansion could require ~$400 million of capital. Given a starting excess capital posture of $560 million, I would expect SF to end 2026 with $500 million of capital.

Shares Are Attractive

This speaks to the strength of the business’s capital generation as well as its excellent starting capital position. SF can easily finance a ~10% growth in its balance sheet and still end the year with nearly as much excess capital as it starts it. In other words, the runway for growth extends well beyond 2026. That leaves shares at just ~13x 2026 earnings. In contrast, Morgan Stanley (MS) trades at about 17x earnings. MS, of course, has a much larger scale, but its revenue breakdown is not that different from SF, and shares trade at a ~30% premium. Some premium is justified, but this appears extreme.

Given its fee-based revenue, I believe SF should trade at least at ~15x revenue, providing upside to about $145, or about 15%, plus its dividend. As such, I view Stifel as a compelling, under-the-radar value stock and recommend buying shares.
